metadata.split_role: Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, chalciporoid, and early oddball bolete records; first half of former mushrooms-boletes-other.json.

records.culinary_uses: Occasional edible when young and firm; not a prime beginner bolete.

staining: Usually not staining; occasionally weak blue bruising on pores

records.edibility_detail: Use only with confident pine-associated long-stemmed bolete ID. Confirm the very long ridged stem, yellow pores, prominent white basal mycelium, and absence of dangerous red-pored traits.
records.field_identification: Mycorrhizal with pines. Look for a reddish-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface, very long ridged to nearly reticulate stem, prominent white basal mycelium, and a cap margin that can project beyond the pore surface.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Aureoboletus projectellus
use_links.notes: Pine association, field marks, season, and staining notes.

records.edibility_notes: Use only with confident pine-associated long-stemmed bolete ID. Confirm the very long ridged stem, yellow pores, prominent white basal mycelium, and absence of dangerous red-pored traits.

edible_use.notes: Use only with confident pine-associated long-stemmed bolete ID. Confirm the very long ridged stem, yellow pores, prominent white basal mycelium, and absence of dangerous red-pored traits.

records.culinary_uses: Not collected for meals in this guide. Keep as an identification record until local use and species limits are checked.

mushroom_profile.summary: Dry, smooth to slightly wrinkled cap; yellow to greenish-yellow pore surface that usually does not bruise; yellowish stem with reddish colorations and tiny dots; flesh may stain bluish in some collections
staining: Pores usually not bruising; flesh may stain bluish
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; species-level local timing needs more checking.

records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Dry, smooth to slightly wrinkled cap; yellow to greenish-yellow pore surface that usually does not bruise; yellowish stem with reddish colorations and tiny dots; flesh may stain bluish in some collections. Reported under hardwoods in eastern North America.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert Boletus key: Aureoboletus roxanae
use_links.notes: Hardwood association, yellow/greenish pores, dotted stem, and non-bruising pore surface.

records.edibility_notes: Yellow-pored, dotted-stem Aureoboletus/Xerocomus-type bolete. Do not infer kitchen use from nearby bolete relatives.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food target; useful mainly as an identification species among cracked-cap and pitted/ridged-spored boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Odd cracked-cap bolete with a brown cap, yellow pores that slowly blue, scaber-like stem texture, and a habit of appearing near decayed oak wood.
research_notes: Check the brown to dark-brown cap that may crack, yellow pores that slowly stain blue then brown, and a stem with brown scaber-like dots or hairy/scaly patches. Often found near or from well-decayed oak wood, which is unusual for a bolete.

records.edibility_detail: Treat as an ID/comparison species rather than a reliable table mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Odd cracked-cap bolete with a brown cap, yellow pores that slowly blue, scaber-like stem texture, and a habit of appearing near decayed oak wood.
records.field_identification: Check the brown to dark-brown cap that may crack, yellow pores that slowly stain blue then brown, and a stem with brown scaber-like dots or hairy/scaly patches. Often found near or from well-decayed oak wood, which is unusual for a bolete.

records.culinary_uses: Not a practical food target; included for recognition and comparison among red cracked-cap boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Reddish cracked-cap bolete with yellow pores that bruise blue and red-toned flesh in the stem; described from Michigan, but still a specialist ID species.
research_notes: Look for a brick-red to reddish cracked cap with pale flesh showing in the cracks, yellow pores that bruise blue then brown, and flesh that is yellow in the cap but reddish in the stem. Microscopic ridged spores separate it from look-alikes.

records.edibility_detail: Treat as a specialist ID species, not a beginner edible.
records.mushroom_card_note: Reddish cracked-cap bolete with yellow pores that bruise blue and red-toned flesh in the stem; described from Michigan, but still a specialist ID species.
records.field_identification: Look for a brick-red to reddish cracked cap with pale flesh showing in the cracks, yellow pores that bruise blue then brown, and flesh that is yellow in the cap but reddish in the stem. Microscopic ridged spores separate it from look-alikes.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as a foraging food in this guide; included for recognition of an odd wood-associated bolete group.

mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus with bluing flesh; use as a taxonomy-aware comparison record, not a food target.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where recorded.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Use for ID/caution only until the exact species concept and local relevance are verified.
records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus with bluing flesh; use as a taxonomy-aware comparison record, not a food target.
records.field_identification: Yellow to yellow-brown bolete on or near pine wood/sawdust or wood-decay situations, with yellow pores/stem, yellow basal mycelium, and flesh that can bruise blue. Compare carefully with Buchwaldoboletus hemichrysus and B. lignicola; taxonomy and names in this group are tangled.
records.taxonomy_note: Reviewed in v4.2.93: treated as Buchwaldoboletus sphaerocephalus in the app, with synonym/complex warning against B. hemichrysus and B. lignicola.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Buchwaldoboletus sphaerocephalus
use_links.notes: Taxon category, synonyms, and public image set.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Buchwaldoboletus hemichrysus
use_links.notes: Comparison group and wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus traits.

records.culinary_uses: Peppery/bitter seasoning-or-curiosity mushroom at best; not a meal mushroom.

mushroom_profile.summary: Peppery reddish-pored bolete like C. piperatus, but with blue bruising and an olive spore print; originally described from Michigan.
research_notes: Small drab pinkish-tan to reddish-brown bolete with reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and peppery/bitter taste; unlike C. piperatus, it can bruise blue and has an olive spore print.

records.edibility_detail: Use only as a careful ID/comparison species. The peppery/bitter taste clue should be nibble-and-spit only, and
records.mushroom_card_note: Peppery reddish-pored bolete like C. piperatus, but with blue bruising and an olive spore print; originally described from Michigan.
records.field_identification: Small drab pinkish-tan to reddish-brown bolete with reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a peppery/bitter taste. Unlike C. piperatus, it can bruise blue and has an olive spore print.
records.edibility_notes: Use only as a careful ID/comparison species. The peppery/bitter taste clue should be nibble-and-spit only, and

records.culinary_uses: Peppery seasoning mushroom at most; not a bulk food.

mushroom_profile.summary: Classic peppery bolete: dull reddish-brown cap, reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a sharp peppery taste.
research_notes: Look for a small drab reddish-brown to pinkish-tan bolete with brownish-red pores that darken but do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a very peppery taste. Usually under conifers but not exclusively.

records.edibility_detail: Taste testing should be nibble-and-spit only. Confirm dull reddish-brown cap, brownish-red pores that do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and very peppery taste.
records.mushroom_card_note: Classic peppery bolete: dull reddish-brown cap, reddish pores, yellow basal mycelium, and a sharp peppery taste.
records.field_identification: Look for a small drab reddish-brown to pinkish-tan bolete with brownish-red pores that darken but do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and a very peppery taste. Usually under conifers but not exclusively.
records.edibility_notes: Taste testing should be nibble-and-spit only. Confirm dull reddish-brown cap, brownish-red pores that do not blue, yellow basal mycelium, and very peppery taste.
